title: Wrap container into single binary using podman
body:
/kind feature

Once in a while I need to do this.
```
podman run -v "$(pwd)":"/root/app":Z -w "/root/app" -it heroku/heroku:18-build
```
Basically regenerate a single `Gemfile.lock` file in current directory. And I want to delegate the task to somebody else. And I want to make it a single binary, for example, `regen`, so that the person could just download and run `regen` without additional `apt-get/choco/dnf install/update/etc` hassle.

Is that possible to build such binary with `podman`? That will run in userspace and won't create any additional files outside of the current project dir.

comments.body:
If you mean could you bundle up everything that is podman along with this command into a single binary, the answer is no.

Podman relies on other packages to run containers.  It requires a containers monitor (conmon) It uses an OCI Runtime (runc, crun, kata).  If you run rootless, it also needs slirp4netns, fuse-overlayfs.  It uses the containers-common package for most of its configuration also.

to me this sounds like you shouild make your own image and push it to a registry.  then use podman container runlabel to build the gem.

https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/docs/source/markdown/podman-container-runlabel.1.md

>  It requires a containers monitor (conmon) It uses an OCI Runtime (runc, crun, kata). If you run rootless, it also needs slirp4netns, fuse-overlayfs. It uses the containers-common package for most of its configuration also.

Is it possible to decouple this to run completely in userspace? If not, what `kernel` interfaces should be supported out of the box to make the user OS "portable podman friendly"?
I really do not understand what you are after.  Any OCI Image is podman friendly and should run on any Linux OS that has podman.

@baude nice feature, thanks. Although **-v /:/host** makes me nervous about trusting Docker Hub. :D
```
@abitrolly you should not trust images period.  But doing -v/:/host gives them full reign on your host.
@rhatdan I want to ensure that any Linux DevOps can run that, like any other tool written in Go and compiled into single binary. Not every Linux OS has `podman`.

Well that is the rub.  You need a container engine to run a container image.  (Podman, Docker, CRI-O, Containerd, Buildah...)
Any one of these can run an image created by Podman, but one of them is required.
@rhatdan so no containers in userspace without some engine one leg in `kernel`? I had a hope that Linux containers got a leg in userspace finally. :D
@rhatdan A little out of topic, but are there any plans to release podman runtime for both Windows/Mac anytime soon?

Answered in other places. NO windows or MAC native support.  Not going to happen.

@abitrolly you need a tool to pull the image, store it and configure the linux kernel to run it.  So yes you need an engine.  You could build a tool to embed all of this into one executable, but no one has done that.
I am going to close this since it is not likely to happen.
Linux kernel is unlikely to run in userspace as a container without additional software?
